entries.body: First people (Paleoindians) move into Maine after the Laurentide ice sheet retreats. Tundra and open spruce parkland environment, similar to Labrador today. The shoreline of the time is now ~200 feet underwater.
Earliest shell middens (mounded deposits of clam, oyster, and mussel shells from Wabanaki coastal camps) preserved along the central Maine coast. Older coastal sites are now submerged.
Wabanaki living in a natural world very similar to today's Maine.
People begin using fired clay pottery (start of the Ceramic Period).
Birchbark canoes in widespread use.
Pope Nicholas V issues the papal bull Dum Diversas declaring war against all non-Christians — foundation of the Doctrine of Discovery.
Samuel de Champlain and Pierre Dugua de Monts sail past Mount Desert Island. Champlain names it Île des Monts Déserts ("island of the bare/lonely mountains").
Henry Hudson's crew aboard the Half Moon bombards and loots a Wabanaki village on the Penobscot River.
French Jesuits aboard the Jonah found Saint Sauveur somewhere in Frenchman Bay near MDI, after being invited by Asticou, the local Wabanaki sakom (chief).
English Captain Samuel Argall destroys Saint Sauveur after less than three months.
A pandemic later called "The Great Dying" wipes out over 75 percent of the inhabitants along the New England coast.
Queen Anne's War. Massachusetts and other colonial governments offer cash bounties for the scalps of Native Americans throughout the conflict.
French and Indian War. Captain Israel Higgins's father, Israel Sr., fights as a British subject.
The Phips Proclamation declares the Penobscot "enemies, rebels and traitors" and offers cash bounties for the scalps of Penobscot men, women, and children.
In the aftermath of the Seven Years' War, the English claim all Wabanaki lands "by right of conquest." English settlement spreads along the Maine coast.
Israel and Mary Higgins — the parents of Captain Israel Higgins — become the first European settlers in present-day Bar Harbor.
American Revolutionary War begins. Frederick Alley's grandfather Christopher Bartlett settles on what is now Bartlett's Island.

Captain Israel Higgins, born on Mount Desert Island in the middle of the Revolution; lost at sea aboard the schooner Julia Ann off Sandy Hook in March 1823. The story of his line is on the Higgins family page.
Battle of Bagaduce / Penobscot Expedition.
Revolutionary War ends. Captain Samuel Hull settles Hull's Cove; his daughter Polly will marry Captain Israel Higgins.
George Washington becomes the first U.S. president.

George Washington and his administration formally promised to protect Native American lands through a series of treaties and the Trade and Intercourse Act of 1790. However, these promises were frequently broken.
Tobias Lord Roberts Sr., founder of the Agamont House and Bar Harbor's first wharf. The full story of the family he built is on the Roberts family page.
Polly ("Mary") Hull Higgins — daughter of Captain Samuel Hull, wife of Captain Israel Higgins — dies at age 36, two months before her husband's father and nine months before her husband himself.
Maine becomes a state.

Johnson v. M'Intosh — the Supreme Court (Chief Justice John Marshall) solidifies the Doctrine of Discovery into U.S. law, ruling that Indigenous peoples have only a "right of occupancy," not legal title to their lands.
Four Penobscot Townships are "sold" by the State of Maine.
Frederick Jarvis Alley, born on Bartlett's Island, builder of the St. Sauveur Hotel, husband of Irene Roberts. Story on the Alley family page.
Irene Ophelia Roberts Alley, daughter of Tobias Roberts and matriarch of the St. Sauveur. Story on the Alley family page.
Lucretia Douglass — twelve years old when she died in a fall on Newport Mountain. Her story is on the Douglass family page.
Hudson River School artists come to the island. Frederic Edwin Church paints "Sunset, Bar Harbor" in 1854.
entries.imageCredit: Frederic Edwin Church, "Sunset, Bar Harbor" (1854). Olana State Historic Site, NY. Public domain.

entries.body: Passamaquoddies gain a nonvoting representative in the Maine state legislature.

Juliette Nickerson — born in the first house built in Bar Harbor; lived her entire life in the village; an early adopter of bobbed hair, "mannish" clothing, and the typewriter; died at eighty-six. Story on her page.
Tobias Roberts Sr. opens The Agamont Hotel.
American Civil War.
Elmer Ellsworth Wasgatt, named for the first Union officer killed in the Civil War. He outlived his father by fourteen years and never reached sixteen. Story on the Wasgatt family page.
Wabanaki encampments rotate among six different locations in Bar Harbor — most commonly the foot of Bridge Street, Albert Meadow, and the end of Ledgelawn Avenue ("Squaw Hollow").
Elmer Wasgatt's father, Benjamin R. Wasgatt of the 26th Maine, dies of consumption in a New Orleans military hospital.
Roberts builds a Bar Harbor wharf, boosting tourism.
St. Sauveur Hotel built by Frederick Alley.
Disease outbreak leads Bar Harbor to modernize water and sewage systems.
St. Sauveur Hotel burns and is rebuilt.
Grover Cleveland, U.S. president (1st term).
Diphtheria kills Aquaie and Ophelia Alley.
The richest Americans arrive: Rockefellers, Morgans, Astors.
Grover Cleveland, U.S. president (2nd term).
Wabanaki encampments banned from the Bar Harbor shoreline.
Irene Ophelia Alley dies. Bar Harbor Civil War monument erected.
Frederick Jarvis Alley dies.
Mount Desert Island recognized as a National Park (originally Lafayette National Park, later renamed Acadia in 1929). Woodrow Wilson president.
Diphtheria toxoid vaccine developed by Gaston Ramon.
Juliette Nickerson dies at eighty-six.
St. Sauveur Hotel demolished.
The Great Fires destroy many of Bar Harbor's historic homes and hotels, changing the town forever.
Maine grants reservation Indians the right to vote in federal elections — Maine was the last state to do so.
